1: ve'eleh shemot bnei Yisrael (wĕʾēlleh šĕmôt bĕnê yiśrāʾēl): 'And these are the names of the sons of Israel.' The Hebrew title of the book is Shemot (Names), not Exodus — names signal covenantal identity, and the whole book is about the people named by YHWH. The opening ve'eleh (and these) links to Genesis's ending, continuing the one narrative of YHWH's covenantal family. Each name listed is a covenant heir whose descendants YHWH will redeem.
2: Reuven Shimon Levi viYehudah: The listing of the twelve sons mirrors the tribal registers of Genesis 46 and Numbers 1. The genealogical record is a covenant document — listing those to whom YHWH made promises (Gen 12; 15; 17; 26; 28). Legal genealogies in the ancient Near East established inheritance rights; this genealogy establishes the covenant heirs who have a claim on YHWH's promise to the patriarchs.
3: Yissachar Zevulun uVinyamin: The continuation of the tribal list through the Leah-sons and Benjamin. Each name embeds a genealogical story from Genesis — Issachar (reward, Gen 30:18), Zebulun (honor/dwell, Gen 30:20), Benjamin (son of the right hand, Gen 35:18). The compression of the patriarchal narratives into a bare list at the Exodus opening shows that the Exodus is the continuation and fulfillment of what God began with the patriarchs.
4: Dan veNaftali Gad ve'Asher: The sons of Bilhah and Zilpah complete the twelve. The full tribal catalog before the oppression narrative establishes that all twelve are equally heirs of the covenant — there is no partial Israel. YHWH's redemption will not leave any tribe behind; the Exodus is for the whole covenant people, not a favored subset.
5: vayhi kol-nefesh yotzei yerech-Yaakov shivim nefesh (wayhî kol-nepeš yōṣĕʾê yereḵ-yaʿăqōb šibʿîm nāpeš): 'All the persons who came from Jacob's loins were seventy souls.' Nefesh (H5315, soul/person/life-force) — each member of the covenant community is a nefesh, a living being bearing the image of God (Gen 2:7 — man became a living nefesh). Seventy is a significant symbolic number in the OT (seventy nations in Gen 10; seventy elders of Israel in Exod 24:1; the seventy years of exile in Jer 29:10).
6: vayamot Yosef vekhol-echav vekhol hador hahu: 'Joseph died, and all his brothers, and all that generation.' The transition from the patriarchal era to the oppression: the generation that knew Joseph and his saving role dies. The forgetting of Joseph by the new Pharaoh (v.8) is thus not merely political ingratitude but the erasure of covenantal memory. When the people who witnessed God's saving acts die without passing on the memory, the covenant community becomes vulnerable — a warning for every generation.
7: vayifru vayishretzu vayirbu vayaattsemu bim'od m'od (wayyipĕrû wayyišrĕṣû wayyirbû wayyaʿaṣmû bimĕʾōd mĕʾōd): 'The Israelites were fruitful and swarmed and multiplied and grew exceedingly strong.' Six verbs in rapid succession — the most concentrated description of population growth in the OT. Sharatz (H8317, swarm/teem) is the word used in Gen 1:20 for the swarming of creatures in the sea — Israel's multiplication is deliberately compared to the original creation-flourishing. The Abrahamic promise (Gen 17:2 — I will multiply you exceedingly) is fulfilled against the odds: vatimale ha'aretz otam (the land was filled with them) echoes Gen 1:28 (fill the earth).
8: vayaqam melek chadash al-Mitsrayim asher lo-yada et-Yosef (wayyāqom melek ḥādāš ʿal-miṣrāyim ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿ ʾet-yôsēp): 'A new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.' Lo-yada (did not know): yada (H3045) is covenantal intimacy and relational recognition, not merely factual awareness. The new Pharaoh does not merely lack information about Joseph — he refuses to recognize the covenantal reality that Joseph represented. Political amnesia as a form of covenant-blindness: deliberately not-knowing what connects the powerful to their obligations.
9: vayomer el-ammo hinei am bnei Yisrael rav ve'atsum mimenu: 'He said to his people: the people of the children of Israel are too numerous and too mighty for us.' Rav ve'atsum (numerous and mighty): the same terms used in v.7 for Israel's growth — Pharaoh's fear response perfectly mirrors what YHWH's blessing produced. The blessing of the covenant people becomes the pretext for their oppression. This is the recurring OT pattern: the flourishing of YHWH's covenant community provokes imperial anxiety.
10: hava nithachkema lo (hābāh niṯḥakkĕmāh-llô): 'Come, let us deal wisely/shrewdly with them.' Nithachkema (Hitpael of chakam, H2449, be wise): the deliberative reflexive — the council of empire deliberating cunning strategy against the covenant people. The supreme irony: Pharaoh's wisdom (chakam) is the anti-type of true wisdom (Prov 9:10: the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom). The wisest nation on earth devises a plan that ultimately destroys itself, while the ones it tries to suppress multiply further (v.12).
11: vayasimu alav sarei missim (wayyāśîmû ʿālāw śārê missîm): 'They appointed over them forced-labor taskmasters.' Missim (H4522, forced labor/tribute levies) is the specific term for the corvee system of imperial labor conscription. Sarei missim (supervisors of forced labor) are the administrative machinery of oppression. Building Pitom and Ra'amses (store-cities) — the wealth of empire built on the bodies of covenant people. The counter-image is Paul's church-as-temple being built with living stones (1 Pet 2:5) through willing participation.
12: ka'asher ye'anu oto ken yirbeh viken yifrotz (kaʾăšer yĕʿannû ʾōtô kēn yirbeh wĕḵēn yiproṣ): 'The more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and spread.' Anah (H6031, afflict/humble/oppress) — the same root used for the affliction YHWH predicted to Abraham (Gen 15:13: your descendants will be ani in a land not theirs). The covenant curse of oppression paradoxically fuels the covenant blessing of multiplication. Yifrotz (spread, H6555) — breaking out/spreading rapidly — the same root used when Jacob's prosperity broke out (Gen 30:43) and when the Davidic covenant promises spread (2 Sam 5:20).
13: vaya'avidu Mitsrayim et-bnei Yisrael befarech (wayyaʿăbidû miṣrāyim ʾet-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl bĕpāreḵ): 'Egypt made the Israelites serve with crushing labor.' Farech (H6531, crushing labor/harshness): the specific term for the severity of forced labor — occurring only here and in Lev 25:43, 46, 53, where YHWH explicitly forbids Israelites from imposing farech on one another, because they know what it is like. The Exodus becomes the ethical foundation for Israel's labor laws.
14: vayemareru et-chayyeihem ba'avoda kasha (waymārărû ʾet-ḥayyêhem bāʿăbōdāh qāšāh): 'They made their lives bitter with hard labor.' Marar (H4843, bitter/make bitter): the same root as the bitter waters of Marah (Exod 15:23), as Naomi's name-change to Mara (Ruth 1:20), and as the bitter herbs of the Passover (Exod 12:8). The bitterness of Egypt is ritually remembered in every Passover celebration through the maror — the physical taste of oppression kept alive in covenant memory.
15: vayomer melek Mitsrayim lameyaldot ha'ivriyot: 'The king of Egypt said to the Hebrew midwives.' The first named women in the Exodus narrative: Shiphrah and Puah — their names preserved while Pharaoh's is not. Meyaldot (midwives, from yalad H3205, bear/give birth): women who stand at the boundary between life and death, facilitating birth against the decree of death. The politics of empire confronts the politics of the womb.
16: biyaldechem et-haivriyot ure'iten al-ha'avnayim: 'When you deliver the Hebrew women, look at the birthstool.' Ha'avnayim (H70, birthstool/two stones): possibly a dual form of even (stone), referring to the two stones or bricks on which a woman crouched for birth in the ancient Near East — attested in Egyptian birth scenes. The intimate setting of delivery is where Pharaoh demands that his death-decree be executed: infanticide disguised as midwifery.
17: vatirena hameyaldot et-haElohim velo asu ka'asher diber aleihen melek Mitsrayim: 'The midwives feared God and did not do as the king of Egypt commanded.' Yirat haElohim (fear of God) vs. compliance with Pharaoh's command — the foundational ethical conflict of the Exodus. The midwives' yirat Elohim is the same phrase used for Abraham's faith (Gen 22:12: now I know you fear God) and the ethic of the stranger (Gen 20:11: there is no fear of God in this place). To fear God is to resist idolatrous human power.
18: vayiqra melek Mitsrayim lameyaldot vayomer lahen madua asitem hadavar hazeh: 'The king of Egypt called the midwives and said: why have you done this thing?' The imperial interrogation of those who defied the decree. The midwives' answer — the Hebrew women are vigorous and give birth before we arrive (v.19) — is a deflection that preserves life. Civil disobedience that protects the innocent and deceives the oppressor is honored here: God blessed the midwives (v.20-21).
19: vatomernu hameyaldot el-Paroh ki lo kaNashim haMitsriyot haivriyot ki chayyot henah: 'The Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women; they are vigorous (chayyot) and give birth before the midwife comes.' Chayyot (H2422, lively/vigorous, or possibly: like animals — they give birth easily): the midwives' response uses the vitality of the covenant people as their defense. The word chayyot is from chayyah (living/life) — the Hebrews' vitality is a mark of divine blessing.
20: vayyeitav Elohim lameyaldot: 'God dealt well with the midwives.' The divine reward for those who fear God over imperial power: vayyeitav (was good to, from tov) — the goodness of God expressed in covenant blessing. The midwives received batim (households/families) — a covenantal blessing of progeny and stability, the very thing they had protected for others. Those who protect life receive life.
21: vayhi ki-yareyu hameyaldot et-haElohim vayaas lahem batim: 'Because the midwives feared God, he gave them households.' The bayit (H1004, house/household) given to the midwives is simultaneously a domestic family unit and a dynastic legacy — the same word used for the house of David (2 Sam 7:11: YHWH will make you a house). Covenant faithfulness under persecution generates covenantal legacy. The midwives who preserved Israel's houses received houses of their own.
22: kol-haben hayilod hayorah tashlichuhu: 'Every son who is born, throw him into the Nile.' Pharaoh's final decree — a public, universal death-sentence on Hebrew male children. The Nile, Egypt's source of life, is made the instrument of death. The anti-creation decree (Gen 1:28: be fruitful and multiply) is reversed: all that is born (yilod) is cast away. The decree anticipates and inverts the Passover: Egypt will lose its firstborn as it tried to kill Israel's sons. The measure used will be measured back (Matt 7:2).
